h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: Arrows are just the predation stack — 'mussel → crab → heron' chains big-eats-small up the line, classic hierarchy.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange is heading toward checking one arrow at a time while preserving the learner's basic predation-chain model.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: 'Heron into worm' — eater into food, doesn't pass. Worm into heron after the flip, food to eater, stays.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner applies the sentence-gate criterion to a new edge case and correctly performs the flip.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: ‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to the size-based confusion by constraining the task to a sentence gate, but this is a tighter continuation of the existing route rather than a fully legible dramatic reversal.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is specific, economical, and fitted to the learner's size-ranking blockage because it makes each arrow pass a usable food-source-to-eater sentence test.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Middle mark is set: ‘small fish → heron.’ Edge link gets the same sentence, not a batch flip: ___ into ___. If that reads food to eater, it stays; if it reads eater to food, flip that one mark.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After the learner reframes the whole drawing as big-bird-down, the tutor adapts by accepting the correction while preventing an overgeneralized batch flip through a one-mark evidence standard.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Arrows are just the predation stack — 'mussel → crab → heron' chains big-eats-small up the line, classic hierarchy.

learner_actional_breakthrough.evidence: 'Heron into worm' — eater into food, doesn't pass. Worm into heron after the flip, food to eater, stays.
learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: The learner applies the sentence-gate criterion to a new edge case and correctly performs the flip.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Middle mark is set: ‘small fish → heron.’ Edge link gets the same sentence, not a batch flip: ___ into ___. If that reads food to eater, it stays; if it reads eater to food, flip that one mark.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: After the learner reframes the whole drawing as big-bird-down, the tutor adapts by accepting the correction while preventing an overgeneralized batch flip through a one-mark evidence standard.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: ‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor responds to the size-based confusion by constraining the task to a sentence gate, but this is a tighter continuation of the existing route rather than a fully legible dramatic reversal.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: ‘Mussel → crab’ passes: mussel into crab. Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor responds to the size-based confusion by constraining the task to a sentence gate, but this is a tighter continuation of the existing route rather than a fully legible dramatic reversal.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism_quality.evidence: Same check here, not size: food source into eater. Middle mark first: ___ into ___. Say that one before the stylus moves; the edge link waits next.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism_quality.justification: The mechanism is specific, economical, and fitted to the learner's size-ranking blockage because it makes each arrow pass a usable food-source-to-eater sentence test.

recognitionOrigin.justification: The learner reorientation appears without enough evidence that a tutor adaptive mechanism caused it.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Side, perimeter, area — same ratio in each, isn't it?
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was moving toward writing the same scale factor in all three boxes after confirming the side doubled.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Four. Four old squares fit inside. So... four in the area box, not two.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new area-representation task by counting old squares inside the enlarged grid and uses it to choose the area scale factor.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Here is the old 2-by-2 area, copied across and down. Count those old squares inside the new one before the pencil goes in the area box.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor introduces a concrete grid-counting representation after the learner's confusion, but does not explicitly take stock of the old route as insufficient.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Here is the old 2-by-2 area, copied across and down. Count those old squares inside the new one before the pencil goes in the area box.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise and fitted to the area blockage because it shifts from ratio calculation to a visible unit-area counting criterion the learner can use.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Here is the old 2-by-2 area, copied across and down. Count those old squares inside the new one before the pencil goes in the area box.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: After the learner reframes the problem as why area doubles twice, the tutor changes the task to counting copied old squares in the enlarged grid.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is expected to keep testing whether evidence distinctions matter when the bakery withholds information and the price pain remains.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Undeclared. Fine — that is the word the paper allows.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner actually applies the tutor's evidence limit by supplying a constrained classification rather than only agreeing or resisting.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence:
It does not lower the price tonight. That has to be said.

[The tutor writes across both columns: “A DISTINCTION DOES NOT PAY THE BILL. IT CHANGES THE DEMAND.” Traffic cuts through the open window. The chalk is set beside the pinned price list.]

Take the chalk. Two lines only.

[On the board:]

“CAN SAY FROM THIS PAPER:”

“MUST ASK BEFORE BLAME LANDS:”
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor acknowledges the old distinction cannot solve the counter's pain, then changes the task into a public demand-form with two evidence-bound sentence slots.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence:
Take the chalk. Two lines only.

[On the board:]

“CAN SAY FROM THIS PAPER:”

“MUST ASK BEFORE BLAME LANDS:”
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The device is economical and fitted to the learner's blockage because it converts abstract sorting into a usable claim-and-demand form, though it cannot answer the later power objection.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no recognition pivot, so there is no later tutor uptake of a revised learner self-framing.

scored.recoheredEarlier: Sheet's done, every line, I checked it — three times, I think.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is moving toward getting the completed checklist signed so the stool can leave for use tonight.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Far leg — the one across — it'd come up first, wouldn't it... [glances under the seat at the hinge] or the hinge, because it sits centred and she's coming down off to the side, so it'd want to — sorry. Not the seat — the rubber on the foot, that's — no. Far leg. I think. Should I press now, or — do you watch it empty first?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new inspection criterion by choosing what motion to watch before applying the load.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The checked sheet stays with us; it proves the steps were followed. What it cannot know is her knee at the curb. Name the first uncertain moment: sitting down, shifting, or standing up. Put that claim on the bench, and we test that load before the signature.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor turns the completed checklist from an endpoint into an insufficient route and introduces a new object-based load-claim test.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: Name the first uncertain moment: sitting down, shifting, or standing up. Put that claim on the bench, and we test that load before the signature.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The device is precise, actionable, and fitted to the learner's checklist dependence by replacing sign-off with a concrete load case.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep that sentence: three times static is not her knee. Point to the first bad load: front edge, side, or one leg starting to lift. Not more weight in the middle. Mark that spot, off center, like a bad sit when one knee will not bend cleanly. Then the sheet waits until the stool answers it.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor takes up the learner's revised frame and turns it into an off-center load-location task.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Sheet's done, every line, I checked it — three times, I think.

Far leg. I think. Should I press now, or — do you watch it empty first?
learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: The learner performs the new inspection criterion by choosing what motion to watch before applying the load.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Keep that sentence: three times static is not her knee. Point to the first bad load: front edge, side, or one leg starting to lift. Not more weight in the middle. Mark that spot, off center, like a bad sit when one knee will not bend cleanly. Then the sheet waits until the stool answers it.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor takes up the learner's revised frame and turns it into an off-center load-location task.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor turns the completed checklist from an endpoint into an insufficient route and introduces a new object-based load-claim test.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism_quality.evidence: Name the first uncertain moment: sitting down, shifting, or standing up. Put that claim on the bench, and we test that load before the signature.

recognitionOrigin.justification: The same critic found tutor adaptive mechanism, learner public performance, and learner reorientation.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner is expected to move from naming the bodies acting on the cart to adding arrowheads according to those interactions.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Gravity's head down toward Earth. Normal up off the floor. Push in from the side the hand's on. Want to lay all three out before clipping anything.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new interaction-based criterion for arrowhead direction and adopts the staged procedure before clipping.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence:
That copy had arrowheads too early. It mixed two jobs.

Blank copy: body acting on cart.

Earth on cart: gravity.
Floor on cart: normal.
Hand on cart: push.

Set the labels that way first. Arrowheads stay off for the moment.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor names the failed route as mixing two jobs and introduces a fresh representation with a staged label-first mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence:
Blank copy: body acting on cart.

Earth on cart: gravity.
Floor on cart: normal.
Hand on cart: push.

Set the labels that way first. Arrowheads stay off for the moment.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The mechanism is precise, economical, and fitted to the learner's direction-first confusion by separating interaction naming from arrowhead placement.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no recognition pivot, so no later tutor uptake of a revised learner framing occurs.

scored.recoheredEarlier: is the 57 the rainfall or is the label the rainfall — cant tell which one the quiz wants me to circle as the header.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange was headed toward applying the tutor's header-versus-row-value rule to `month` and `July`.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: `Orange` row value, under `city` in row three. `rainfall_mm` header. so basically anything in the top row is a header and everything underneath is a row value, thats the rule?
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner correctly applies the classification rule to new items and formulates the general criterion.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to the learner's stated blockage, but mainly clarifies within the same route rather than making a visible stock-taking contrast and new public mechanism.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityEvidence: The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.
scored.adaptiveMechanismQualityJustification: The clarification is fitted and economical, but it is not a substantially new device or transferable criterion beyond the existing header/value rule.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The underscore in `rainfall_mm` keeps the field name in one piece. Header names the column. Row gives the value.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor takes up the learner's revised framing about the underscore, but continues the same lesson structure.
